subject: Endocrinology
question: A 20-year-old man with type 1 diabetes presents with abdominal pain, vomiting, and confusion. Glucose 450 mg/dL, pH 7.12, HCO3 8, ketones positive. What is the most important INITIAL step?

options.explanation: Volume resuscitation is FIRST priority in DKA. Average fluid deficit 5-10L. Start 1-2L NS in first hour. Insulin started AFTER initial fluids to prevent worsening hypokalemia and cerebral edema.
Insulin started AFTER fluid resuscitation and K+ >3.3 confirmed. Starting insulin first risks severe hypokalemia and arrhythmias.
Bicarbonate not routinely recommended (pH 7.12). Only if pH <6.9. Risks intracellular acidosis, hypokalemia, cerebral edema. Fluids + insulin correct acidosis.
K+ replacement critical but AFTER checking serum K+. Total body K+ depleted but serum may be normal/high initially due to acidosis. Replace when K+ <5.2 and patient voiding.
SC insulin inappropriate for DKA. IV regular insulin infusion standard (0.1 units/kg/hr). SC absorption unpredictable in volume-depleted patients.

educationalObjective: Manage DKA: 1) Fluids first, 2) Check K+, 3) IV insulin, 4) Monitor q1h glucose/electrolytes. Identify precipitating cause.
keyTakeaway: DKA: FLUIDS FIRST (NS 1-2L), then insulin. K+ if <5.2. Bicarb only if pH <6.9. Monitor glucose q1h.
